do you understand what just happened?

I think Google's new Stitch tool is a really great example of bringing "vibework" to an area outside of coding with an interface built around design & prototyping. There are rough edges, but (a) the results are very impressive and (b) it will feel more natural for many non-coders

It also shows how general purpose these models are right now, capable of doing many things with different harnesses, so I think we will see similar applications from other labs soon, as more knowledge work becomes the focus of AI. Anyhow, its free for now:

This is genuinely a funny development for articles, given how many of them are obviously AI expansions of a single idea that could be expressed in a tweet.

(There are some excellent articles as well, but now people won’t read those either)

We are back to the phase of the AI news cycle where people are underestimating how jagged the AI ability frontier is, as well as how much they still depend on expert human decision-making or guidance at key points in order to function well. Still far from "doing all jobs," today.

i tested 12 different ways to describe the same n8n workflow to an AI.

9 of them produced broken output. missing nodes. wrong connections. workflows that needed 45 minutes of debugging before they did anything useful.

3 worked first try. no edits. just ran.

the difference

i've watched people spend 6 months learning n8n and still not have a single workflow running in production.

i've watched other people deploy their first automation in an afternoon.

the gap isn't skill. it's not experience.

it's one decision made at the start.

they stopped

10 automations. 10 plain-English prompts. 25+ hours/week saved.

- Form to CRM to welcome email (45 min/day eliminated)
- Invoice reminders on autopilot (£2K/month recovered)
- Google Sheets to scheduled posts across platforms
- New client to Slack + Drive + onboarding in seconds

claude opus 4.6 just replaced every automation consultant i know.

i gave it one paragraph. one.

"go find everyone in the n8n community,
scrape their profiles, enrich their emails,
and send personalized outreach to each one."

it didn't start building. it started thinking.
the tool giving opus 4.6 full n8n access is synta(.)io - it plans, builds, deploys, and self-heals workflows from plain english. no code. no consultant.

most people use AI like google. type a question. get an answer. close the tab.

the builders actually making money right now treat it completely different.

they don't ask AI questions. they give it context, constraints, and a job to do. like onboarding a new hire who happens to

most n8n error handling i see looks like this: one error trigger at the bottom that catches everything.

sounds fine until your third API call fails and you have no idea which one broke or what data already went through.

better pattern: wrap each external call in its own
